q: What are the four Snowflake editions and what key capabilities differentiate each one?
a: Snowflake offers four editions stacked hierarchically: Standard is the entry tier, providing core warehousing, unlimited users, automatic clustering, and semi-structured data support with up to 1 day of Time Travel. Enterprise adds multi-cluster warehouses for concurrency scaling, materialized views, column-level security, periodic rekeying of data, and up to 90 days of Time Travel — it is the most common choice for mid-to-large companies. Business Critical (formerly Enterprise for Sensitive Data / ESD) adds HIPAA and HITRUST CSF compliance, PCI DSS support, customer-managed keys (Tri-Secret Secure), database failover/failback for disaster recovery, and higher-security AWS PrivateLink or Azure Private Link by default. Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) wraps all Business Critical features inside a completely isolated Snowflake environment with dedicated metadata services and compute pools, used by highly regulated institutions such as financial firms where even shared infrastructure is unacceptable.
fj: Business Critical is just Enterprise with PrivateLink added; it doesn't change compliance certifications.
fs: Business Critical is purpose-built for regulated data: it adds HIPAA, HITRUST CSF, PCI DSS certifications, customer-managed encryption (Tri-Secret Secure), and DR failover — not only PrivateLink.

q: A healthcare company stores PHI data in Snowflake and must comply with HIPAA. Which edition is the minimum they need, and what specific features make it compliant?
a: Business Critical Edition is the minimum required for HIPAA compliance on Snowflake. It provides the BAA (Business Associate Agreement) that Snowflake signs with covered entities, AES-256 encryption at rest with optional Tri-Secret Secure (customer holds one of three encryption keys via a cloud KMS, so Snowflake cannot decrypt data without the customer's key), enhanced security auditing, and account failover/failback for business continuity. It also includes AWS PrivateLink and Azure Private Link as a built-in option so PHI never traverses the public internet. Standard and Enterprise editions do not include the BAA or Tri-Secret Secure, making them insufficient for covered entities processing PHI.
fj: Enterprise Edition supports HIPAA because it has column-level security and 90-day Time Travel.
fs: Column-level security and Time Travel are governance features, not compliance certifications. HIPAA requires a BAA, Tri-Secret Secure encryption, and audit controls that are only available at Business Critical and above.

code.label: Check if Tri-Secret Secure is enabled on your account

q: How does Snowflake's Git integration work, and what objects does it create inside a Snowflake account?
a: Snowflake Git integration lets you clone a remote Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps) into a special repository stage inside your Snowflake account. You first create a SECRET object to hold your personal access token, then create an API INTEGRATION that authorises Snowflake to contact the Git provider, and finally execute CREATE OR REPLACE GIT REPOSITORY pointing at the remote URL. Snowflake performs a full clone including all branches and tags. You can reference files in the repository stage (e.g. @my_repo/branches/main/my_proc.py) directly in CREATE PROCEDURE or CREATE FUNCTION statements so the handler code remains in version control. Use ALTER GIT REPOSITORY … FETCH to pull the latest commits without re-creating the object. Access is currently read-only from stored procedures and UDFs; write-back is only supported from Notebooks.
fj: Git integration works by giving Snowflake SSH access to your repo and mounting it as a regular named internal stage.
fs: Snowflake does not use SSH mounts. It creates a dedicated repository stage type via API INTEGRATION and a SECRET object — distinct from regular internal stages — and accesses the repo over HTTPS.

code.label: Set up Git integration
lines: CREATE OR REPLACE API INTEGRATION my_github_integration
CREATE OR REPLACE GIT REPOSITORY my_repo
-- 5. Reference a file in a stored procedure

q: What is AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake and what are the steps to enable it?
a: AWS PrivateLink creates a private network path between your AWS VPC and Snowflake so that all traffic stays on the AWS backbone and never traverses the public internet. Snowflake exposes a VPC endpoint service per region, and you create a VPC Interface Endpoint (VPCE) in your own VPC that connects to it. The high-level steps are: (1) confirm your account is on Business Critical or higher — PrivateLink requires at least that edition; (2) call SYSTEM$GET_PRIVATELINK_CONFIG() in Snowflake to retrieve the regionless privatelink-vpce-id and the Snowflake account URL you should use; (3) in the AWS console, create a VPC Interface Endpoint using the vpce-id as the service name, choosing the subnets and security groups that should reach Snowflake; (4) after the endpoint is accepted, update DNS so your Snowflake account hostname resolves to the private IP, or use the privatelink account URL directly; (5) optionally enforce that all client connections must use PrivateLink by setting the network policy to block public traffic.
fj: PrivateLink is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard.
fs: PrivateLink requires Business Critical Edition or higher. Standard and Enterprise accounts cannot enable PrivateLink.

code.label: Retrieve PrivateLink config from Snowflake
lines: -- Run as ACCOUNTADMIN to get the VPCE service name

code.label: Enforce PrivateLink-only access via network policy
lines: -- Block all non-PrivateLink traffic by creating a network policy
-- that only permits the private VPCE IP range
CREATE OR REPLACE NETWORK POLICY privatelink_only

q: Describe all five Snowflake stage types and when you would choose each.
a: Snowflake has two internal stage categories and three external stage options. The user stage (@~) is automatically provisioned per Snowflake user and is suited for personal, ad-hoc file uploads — it cannot be named or referenced by other users. The table stage (@%table_name) is automatically provisioned per table and is ideal for loading data into that specific table; it disappears when the table is dropped. Named internal stages (CREATE STAGE … without an external URL) are explicitly named, shareable, and support directory tables — they are the standard choice for organised team workflows. Named external stages reference cloud object storage owned by you: S3 (STORAGE_PROVIDER = 'S3'), GCS (STORAGE_PROVIDER = 'GCS'), or Azure Blob / ADLS Gen2 (STORAGE_PROVIDER = 'AZURE'). External stages use a storage integration to avoid embedding credentials and are the right choice when data originates outside Snowflake or when you need to unload data back to cloud storage for downstream consumers.
fj: User stages and table stages must be created explicitly with CREATE STAGE before use.
fs: User stages and table stages are automatically provisioned by Snowflake — they exist by default for every user and every table. Only named internal and external stages require CREATE STAGE.

q: A Snowflake query reading from an external S3 stage is failing with an access denied error. What is the preferred architecture to fix this without embedding static credentials?
a: The recommended approach is a storage integration, a Snowflake account-level object of TYPE = EXTERNAL_STAGE that establishes a trust relationship between Snowflake and your cloud provider using IAM rather than static keys. For S3: you create the storage integration with STORAGE_PROVIDER = 'S3', STORAGE_AWS_ROLE_ARN pointing to an IAM role you control, and STORAGE_ALLOWED_LOCATIONS scoping the allowed buckets. Snowflake generates a unique IAM entity (STORAGE_AWS_IAM_USER_ARN and STORAGE_AWS_EXTERNAL_ID) that you retrieve via DESCRIBE INTEGRATION. You then update the trust policy of your IAM role to allow exactly that ARN and external ID to assume the role — this is the secure handshake. Finally you create or alter the external stage to reference the integration. The stage and all COPY INTO statements through it will use the assumed role, and no credentials are ever stored in Snowflake.
fj: You should store the AWS access key and secret in the stage definition using CREDENTIALS = (AWS_KEY_ID = '...' AWS_SECRET_KEY = '...').
fs: Embedding static credentials is insecure and an anti-pattern; they are visible to users with DESCRIBE STAGE privileges and cannot be rotated automatically. Storage integrations use IAM role assumption, so no long-lived credentials are stored in Snowflake.

code.label: Create S3 storage integration
lines: -- Step 1: create the integration (run as ACCOUNTADMIN)
CREATE OR REPLACE STORAGE INTEGRATION my_s3_integration
-- Step 2: retrieve the IAM user Snowflake generated
-- then update the trust policy of the IAM role with these values.
-- Step 3: create the external stage referencing the integration

q: What is a Snowflake Data Clean Room and how does it enable two companies to analyse overlapping data without exposing raw records?
a: A Snowflake Data Clean Room is a controlled collaboration environment, built on top of Snowflake's secure data sharing and access control primitives, that allows two or more parties to run joint analyses on combined datasets without either party ever seeing the other's raw data. The provider installs a clean room application in the consumer's account (or vice versa) using the Native App Framework; the app exposes only pre-approved SQL templates — for example, an audience overlap query — and enforces rules such as aggregation policies (results must be aggregated, not row-level) and minimum threshold requirements (a result is only returned if the cohort exceeds a set minimum, e.g. 1,000 users). Differential privacy noise can optionally be added. Use cases include retail media (matching a retailer's purchase data with an advertiser's audience), healthcare cohort analysis, and financial fraud detection across institutions — all without a central data broker.
fj: A Data Clean Room works by copying both parties' data into a shared S3 bucket that both can query.
fs: No data is copied to a shared external location. Data stays in each party's own Snowflake account; only query results that satisfy the clean room's privacy constraints are returned through the app's pre-approved templates.

q: A retailer and an ad-tech firm want to measure audience overlap for a co-marketing campaign. They both use Snowflake. Walk through how a Data Clean Room enforces privacy while still producing useful overlap metrics.
a: The retailer (provider) creates a clean room and publishes it to the ad-tech firm (consumer) via the Snowflake Native App Framework or the managed Snowflake Data Clean Rooms UI. The provider shares their hashed customer email table into the clean room but wraps it in a pre-approved template query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM provider_customers WHERE hashed_email IN (SELECT hashed_email FROM consumer_audiences). The clean room enforces an aggregation policy that prevents the query from returning individual rows and a minimum threshold rule (e.g., result must be >= 1,000 to prevent de-anonymisation of small groups). The consumer can run the template and learn that, say, 340,000 of their audience matches — without ever accessing the retailer's raw hashed emails or PII. The provider's data never leaves their Snowflake account; only the aggregate number crosses the boundary. Both parties' raw tables remain governed by their own access controls, and the clean room app logs all template executions for audit.
fj: The clean room shares tables using standard Snowflake data sharing so the consumer can run any SELECT against the provider's data with appropriate role grants.
fs: Standard data sharing grants full SELECT access, allowing the consumer to read raw rows. Clean rooms replace that with query templates and policy enforcement (aggregation, minimum thresholds) so raw data is never accessible, even though the underlying mechanism uses Snowflake sharing primitives.

q: What is the Snowflake Native App Framework, and what are the core objects a provider must create to publish an app to the Snowflake Marketplace?
a: The Snowflake Native App Framework allows developers to build, package, and distribute data applications that run directly inside a consumer's Snowflake account — the app code executes on the consumer's compute, but the provider retains IP protection because business logic in stored procedures and UDFs is encrypted and not readable by the consumer. A provider must create: (1) an APPLICATION PACKAGE — the distributable container that holds shared data and the application code; (2) a setup script (setup.sql) that Snowflake runs automatically when the consumer installs the app, creating databases, schemas, procedures, and Streamlit views; (3) optionally, Snowpark or JavaScript stored procedures for business logic, and Streamlit for interactive UI. The provider publishes the application package as a listing on the Snowflake Marketplace (free or paid). Consumers install it with CREATE APPLICATION and see only the surfaces the provider explicitly exposes. Apps can also use Snowpark Container Services for workloads requiring custom runtimes.
fj: A Native App is just a shared database — the consumer gets access to tables and views but cannot run custom code inside the app.
fs: Native Apps are more than data sharing. They include stored procedures, UDFs, and Streamlit UIs packaged in an APPLICATION PACKAGE with IP protection. The provider's logic runs inside the consumer's account but remains encrypted and inaccessible to the consumer.
